questions.questionText: Which virulence factor of Streptococcus pyogenes is most strongly associated with resistance to phagocytosis?
questions.explanation: M protein is the major antiphagocytic virulence factor of Streptococcus pyogenes and is also linked to rheumatogenic strains. Streptolysin O is a hemolysin. Erythrogenic toxin mediates scarlet fever rash. Hyaluronidase aids spread but is not the main antiphagocytic factor.

questions.questionText: A lactose fermenting, indole positive, motile gram-negative bacillus causing UTI is most likely:
questions.explanation: E. coli is a lactose fermenting, indole-positive, motile gram-negative bacillus and the most common cause of community-acquired UTI. Klebsiella is lactose fermenting but non-motile and urease positive. Proteus is non-lactose fermenting and urease positive. Pseudomonas is non-lactose fermenting and oxidase positive.

questions.questionText: Black colonies on Wilson and Blair medium are characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Salmonella Typhi produces hydrogen sulfide and forms black colonies on bismuth sulfite (Wilson and Blair) medium. Shigella does not produce H2S. Vibrio cholerae grows on TCBS medium. Yersinia has different culture characteristics. This is a classic culture-media association.
